competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd == vs ===
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep question on strict versus loose equality, which is one of the main subtopics in our integrated bug page.
theirStrengths: It is likely strong on the exact interview framing around coercion and equality semantics.
theirGaps: It is narrower than our integrated page because it does not obviously combine equality with falsy defaults and shared mutation in one bug story.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is directly relevant on equality semantics, so the real-world comparison stays close even if our page integrates more than one bug source.
actionableExamples: Its quiz-style examples for `==` and `===` likely keep the example quality competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page adds the combined bug pattern, but GreatFrontEnd still covers one of the main follow-up confusions directly.

realWorldUseCases: Add one explicit integrated bug where a loose comparison and a falsy default fail in the same helper.
actionableExamples: Add one compact before/after code block that combines equality, defaulting, and shared mutation in sequence.
followUpConfusion: Add one note that separates coercion bugs from shared-reference bugs so the integrated answer remains easy to unpack.

competitors.label: MDN Strict equality
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical competitor for strict-equality semantics and object identity, both of which are central subtopics in this integrated bug page.
competitors.winReason: Our page is broader on the integrated bug pattern because it combines defaults, equality, and shared mutation rather than explaining equality in isolation.
theirStrengths: It states strict-equality semantics clearly and correctly, including object identity.
theirGaps: It only covers one part of the integrated bug pattern and does not connect equality to falsy defaults or shared mutation.

realWorldUseCases: This is a high-frequency bug pattern: defaults that overwrite valid falsy values, loose equality coercion surprises, and accidental shared-object mutation.
followUpConfusion: Think of this as three guardrails: default correctly, compare strictly, and protect shared state.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains how `===` behaves, but it does not connect the operator to the multi-step bug pattern seen in real UI code.
actionableExamples: It provides simple equality examples, so the example criterion stays close rather than clearly losing.
followUpConfusion: It does not answer the next question of how equality, falsy defaults, and shared object mutation interact in one bug.

actionableExamples: Keep one integrated before/after code block so the multi-step bug pattern is immediately visible.

competitors.label: GeeksforGeeks Call by Value vs Reference
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a strong competitor for the shared-mutation portion of the integrated bug pattern.
competitors.winReason: Our page is stronger as an integrated debugging guide because it connects shared mutation to falsy-default and equality mistakes instead of teaching reference behavior in isolation.
theirStrengths: It clearly explains primitives by value and objects by shared reference with a mutation example.
theirGaps: It does not connect that reference behavior to falsy-default bugs or equality mistakes in one debugging flow.

realWorldUseCases: This is a high-frequency bug pattern: defaults that overwrite valid falsy values, loose equality coercion surprises, and accidental shared-object mutation.
followUpConfusion: Think of this as three guardrails: default correctly, compare strictly, and protect shared state.

realWorldUseCases: GeeksforGeeks explains shared-object mutation well, but it treats that topic in isolation rather than as one leg of a combined bug pattern.
actionableExamples: Its by-value and by-reference examples are concrete enough that the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page better resolves why a fallback, a loose comparison, and a shared object bug can all appear in the same broken helper.

actionableExamples: Add one slightly more explicit before/after code block where the same function shows all three mistakes in sequence.
